target.title: fix(browser): allow upload from inbound media directory
target.body:
`browser.upload` rejected managed WebChat attachments because upload validation only accepted `DEFAULT_UPLOAD_DIR`. This PR keeps that uploads root and adds the managed inbound media root, including the model-visible `media://inbound/<id>` and `media/inbound/<id>` forms used by WebChat attachment staging.

Fixes #83544

AI-assisted: yes. Codex generated the patch; I reviewed the diff and verified the upload path validation behavior locally.

## Summary

- **Problem:** WebChat attachments are stored as managed inbound media, but `browser.upload` only accepted paths under the temp uploads directory.
- **Why it matters:** An agent that receives a WebChat file attachment can be unable to upload that file into a browser file input.
- **What changed:** Browser upload path validation now uses shared helpers that validate each requested file independently against the uploads directory and then the managed inbound media directory. The helpers also resolve `media://inbound/<id>` and sandbox-relative `media/inbound/<id>` before root validation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Arbitrary local paths are still rejected. Managed media references are limited to a single first-level inbound media id, and the final resolved paths still go through the existing root validators.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83544
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `browser.upload` can now resolve managed inbound media supplied as an absolute inbound path, `media://inbound/<id>`, or sandbox-relative `media/inbound/<id>`, while rejecting paths outside both allowed roots. Mixed multi-file uploads can combine temp upload files and inbound media files in one request.
- Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw checkout, Node v24, branch `fix/browser-upload-inbound-media`, using the browser extension upload path validators and Playwright upload entry tests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/paths.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.set-input-files.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.upload-paths.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.screenshots-element-selector.test.ts --reporter=verbose

OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > resolves canonical inbound media URI references before root validation
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > resolves sandbox-relative inbound media paths before root validation
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > accepts mixed upload-root and inbound-media files in one request
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > rejects nested inbound media URI references
✓ resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths > resolves inbound media URI references for use-time upload validation
✓ resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths > resolves sandbox-relative inbound media paths for use-time upload validation
✓ resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths > accepts mixed upload-root and inbound-media files at use time
✓ armFileUploadViaPlaywright upload path validation > sets files using resolved inbound media paths

Test Files 5 passed (5)
Tests 90 passed (90)
```

- Observed result after fix: Model-visible WebChat media references are normalized to the managed inbound media directory before upload root validation. Each requested file is validated independently, so mixed uploads containing both temp upload files and inbound media files pass when every file belongs to one allowed root. Files outside uploads and inbound media still fail validation; nested/escaped media references are rejected.
- What was not tested: Full live WebChat/Gateway/browser session with a real user attachment. The local proof covers the exact browser upload validation helpers and both Playwright upload entry points that gate the file input behavior.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ClawSweeper review identified that `media://inbound/<id>` and `media/inbound/<id>` still failed because the previous helper retried only the raw requested path under the physical inbound directory, and later identified mixed-root multi-file validation as missing.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The upload action originally hardcoded the temp uploads directory. The first fix added the physical inbound media directory but did not resolve the managed media reference forms WebChat exposes to agents, and validated the whole file list against one root at a time.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing upload tests mocked path validation and did not cover model-visible WebChat media references, mixed-root multi-file uploads, or the file chooser use-time validation path.
- Contributing context (if known): Browser upload, WebChat attachment storage, and sandbox media staging are separate surfaces with different path forms.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/paths.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.upload-paths.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.set-input-files.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Upload validation accepts managed inbound media references and sandbox-relative media paths, supports mixed uploads from both allowed roots, rejects nested media refs, rejects paths outside both roots, and revalidates at Playwright use-time.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the shared upload resolver and both browser file-input entry points without requiring a live Gateway session.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None before this PR.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

WebChat file attachments can be passed to `browser.upload` using the managed inbound media references that agents see in prompts, not only hidden host absolute paths. Multi-file uploads can combine normal temp upload files with managed inbound media files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[WebChat attachment] -> media://inbound/report.pdf -> browser.upload -> uploads root check -> reject
[mixed files] -> [uploads/a.txt, media://inbound/b.txt] -> one-root batch validation -> reject

After:
[WebChat attachment] -> media://inbound/report.pdf -> managed inbound resolver -> inbound root validation -> upload
[WebChat attachment] -> media/inbound/report.pdf -> managed inbound resolver -> inbound root validation -> upload
[mixed files] -> per-file root validation -> upload
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? Yes
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: The upload allowlist now includes managed inbound media in addition to the existing uploads directory. The mitigation is narrow parsing: only `media://inbound/<single-id>`, `media/inbound/<single-id>`, and files that validate inside the managed inbound directory are accepted. Nested references, path traversal, symlinks, hardlinks, and files outside both roots remain rejected by the existing validators.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node v24, local OpenClaw checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WebChat attachment path forms -> browser upload validation
- Relevant config (redacted): Default OpenClaw config; no secrets used

### Steps

1. Create test files under fixture upload and inbound media directories.
2. Resolve upload paths for `media://inbound/report.pdf`, `media/inbound/report.pdf`, and a mixed request containing both upload-root and inbound-media files through `resolveExistingUploadPaths` and `resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths`.
3. Exercise the `browser.upload`, `setInputFilesViaPlaywright`, and `armFileUploadViaPlaywright` upload validation paths.

### Expected

- Managed inbound media references resolve to the inbound media file and pass upload validation.
- Mixed upload-root and inbound-media file lists pass when every path belongs to one allowed root.
- Nested media references and paths outside both allowed roots are rejected.

### Actual

- Matches expected in the focused local tests: 90/90 passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: uploads root fallback, inbound absolute path fallback, `media://inbound/<id>`, `media/inbound/<id>`, mixed upload-root plus inbound-media file lists, nested media reference rejection, outside-root rejection, file chooser use-time validation, direct `setInputFiles` validation.
- Edge cases checked: nested encoded URI path (`media://inbound/nested%2Fsecret.pdf`), missing file rejection in strict validation, outside path rejection.
- What you did **not** verify: A full live WebChat attachment flow through Gateway into a real browser file input.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Browser upload can now select files from managed inbound media.
  - Mitigation: Only OpenClaw-managed inbound media references and files passing existing root validation are accepted; arbitrary local paths remain rejected.

Generated with Codex.

target.title: Doctor: convert interactive maintenance checks
target.body:
## Goal

Start the repair-side migration for structured `openclaw doctor` checks with the first interactive maintenance repairs, while moving Doctor toward the intended split: `openclaw doctor --lint` for read-only findings and `openclaw doctor --fix` for mutation. This PR also builds the shared support that lets `--fix --dry-run` preview repair effects without mutating local state and lets `--fix --diff` report textual diffs when a repair can produce them.

The structured health-check contract already gives doctor a read-only detection shape:

```ts
detect(ctx, scope?) -> HealthFinding[]
repair(ctx, findings) -> HealthRepairResult
```

This PR makes that contract do real repair work for checks that prompt, mutate local state, report effects, support dry-run previews, and need post-repair validation. The important contract point is that `detect()` remains the read-only source of truth, `repair()` receives the exact findings it may fix, and repair output is machine-useful through `changes`, `warnings`, `diffs`, and `effects`.

Notes are threaded through like the rest of OpenClaw: presentation only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; no `detect().notes`, no `repair().notes`, and no runner-owned note arrays.

## Converted Here

This PR migrates the first interactive maintenance checks into structured doctor repairs:

- shell completion health and repair
- channel startup maintenance repair
- systemd user linger detection and repair for local Gateway
- presentation-only doctor notes for Claude CLI, browser readiness, and workspace suggestions

It also adds the doctor context needed by converted checks: env forwarding, doctor options, confirm prompts, note sink, dry-run, and diff flags.

## Contract Examples In This PR

The converted checks all follow the same shape:

```ts
detect(ctx, scope?) -> HealthFinding[]
repair(ctx, findings) -> {
  changes?: string[]
  warnings?: string[]
  diffs?: HealthRepairDiff[]
  effects?: HealthRepairEffect[]
  status?: "repaired" | "skipped" | "failed"
}
```

Concrete examples from this round:

| Check | Detects | Repairs | Dry-run / diff / effect proof |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `core/doctor/shell-completion` | shell completion health through the existing completion detector; lint forces non-interactive mode | runs the existing shell completion repair path with the existing confirm hook | `doctor --fix --dry-run --diff` reports a `would-repair-shell-completion` effect and an empty `diffs` array, because the repair is a profile/cache side effect rather than a textual config edit |
| `core/doctor/startup-channel-maintenance` | only contributes a repair finding during `doctor --fix`, preserving the old maintenance pass shape | runs channel startup maintenance through the structured repair runner | `doctor --fix --dry-run --diff` reports `would-run-channel-startup-maintenance` as an effect and no textual diff, matching the legacy non-file side effect |
| `core/doctor/systemd-linger` | local Linux Gateway service is loaded and user linger is disabled | calls the existing systemd linger repair path with confirmation | `doctor --fix --dry-run --diff` reports `would-enable-systemd-linger` as an effect and no textual diff; the late legacy post-gateway-services pass remains for ordering compatibility |
| `core/doctor/workspace-suggestions` | no lint finding; this is presentation-only information | no repair | no dry-run diff or effect, because there is no repair; emits through `ctx.doctor.note(...)` only when a doctor note sink exists |

These examples are intentionally small but cover the contract surfaces the rest of the stack needs: read-only detection, finding-scoped repair, prompt forwarding, dry-run without mutation, `--diff` output that distinguishes textual diffs from non-file side effects, and post-repair validation by rerunning detection. In this PR the converted repairs are mostly side effects, so the correct `--diff` behavior is explicit effects plus empty textual diffs rather than fake patches.

## Invariants For This Conversion

The conversion is intentionally conservative. Each moved repair should stay isomorphic with the legacy path: same detection conditions, same prompts where prompts already existed, same repair ordering, same side effects, and the same user-facing notes unless a test proves a deliberate cleanup.

The bar for each converted check is:

- preserve backward-compatible doctor ordering, especially for positional repair passes
- keep `doctor` and `doctor --lint` read-only except for existing presentation notes
- keep `doctor --fix` behavior compatible while routing mutation through `repair(ctx, findings)`
- add focused tests for detect, repair, dry-run, diff/effects output, and detect-after validation
- prove that presentation-only notes stay on the existing note sink instead of becoming findings or repair payloads

That is the model the follow-on repair PRs should copy.

## Review Surface

This is the contract slice to challenge before the rest of the stack builds on it:

- whether `repair(ctx, findings)` is the right repair boundary for converted doctor checks
- whether detect-after validation should run after every successful non-dry-run repair
- whether dry-run, diff output, and knowing what will happen before `doctor --fix` mutates state are important enough to make them part of this repair contract
- whether notes should stay purely presentation-only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`
- whether systemd linger should remain available both as an early structured check and as the later legacy post-gateway-services pass

## Follow-On Stack

This is the base PR for the current doctor repair stack:

| PR | Area |
| --- | --- |
| #84290 | plugin registry repairs |
| #84326 | sandbox repairs |
| #84340 | gateway service repairs |
| #84366 | session state repairs |
| #84450 | cron/config audit repairs |
| #84472 | dry-run preview reports |

Those PRs should build on this contract instead of reintroducing per-flow repair notes, ad hoc lint output, or local presentation models.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts src/flows/doctor-startup-channel-maintenance.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-83753-rebase.tsbuildinfo`

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: This PR starts the structured Doctor repair migration by applying finding-scoped repair, machine-readable repair results, presentation-only notes through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`, dry-run effect previews, and detect-after validation to the first interactive maintenance repairs.

Real environment tested: WSL source checkout at commit `ab8f18e2d8c412974ad816edb80b74cede1789d0`, rebased on `origin/main` commit `2c0c9c92f4`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts src/flows/doctor-startup-channel-maintenance.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-83753-rebase.tsbuildinfo`

Evidence after fix: 7 focused Doctor test files passed, 61 tests total, covering structured repair execution, repair filtering, positional repair ordering, dry-run effects, diff propagation, presentation-only notes, shell completion lint behavior, systemd linger repair behavior, and detect-after repair validation.

Observed result after fix: The rebased PR is mergeable against current `main`. Converted repair paths expose findings and repair outputs only; human notes flow through the existing suppressible note system when a doctor runner provides `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; lint remains findings-only.

What was not tested: Full platform-wide doctor scenarios and the entire stacked repair series were not rerun locally for this conflict-resolution refresh; those should run through normal PR CI across the stack.

target.title: fix(agents): secure debug env var access and add validation
target.body:
## Problem
Debug environment variables like OPENCLAW_DEBUG_INGRESS_TIMING could be 
accidentally enabled in production, potentially exposing sensitive timing 
information or increasing attack surface.

## Solution

## Summary

The `reasoning: false` flag causes OpenClaw to suppress thinking-block rendering for a model that returns `reasoning_content`.

target.title: fix(memory-lancedb): stop forwarding embedding dimensions upstream
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `memory-lancedb` forwarded `dimensions` to the OpenAI embeddings request, which breaks vLLM and other non-Matryoshka providers that reject that field.
- Why it matters: memory recall/search can fail at embed time for compatible providers that do not accept `dimensions`, so the plugin stops working for those configs.
- What changed: the embeddings client now sends only `model` and `input` upstream, then truncates the returned vector locally to the configured dimension.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): config shape, provider selection, secrets handling, and runtime permissions are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes none
- Related none
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: the embedding wrapper assumed OpenAI-compatible providers would accept `dimensions`, then passed it straight through upstream.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test proving the outbound request omits `dimensions` and that truncation happens locally.
- Contributing context (if known): this only fails on providers that do not support the `dimensions` field, so the bug is easy to miss if testing only against OpenAI-style embeddings.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: [extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts](extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts)
- Scenario the test should lock in: when `dimensions` is configured, the outbound OpenAI request must not include `dimensions`, the returned vector must be truncated locally, and short vectors must fail clearly.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the exact embedding wrapper that used to forward the bad field and proves the post-response truncation behavior directly.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): the new regression tests in [extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts](extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts)
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Memory recall now works with providers that reject `dimensions` in the embedding request, as long as they return a vector long enough to truncate locally.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
memory_recall -> OpenAI request with dimensions -> provider error

After:
memory_recall -> OpenAI request without dimensions -> receive full embedding -> local truncation -> store/search
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows
- Runtime/container: local workspace with pnpm
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible embeddings / vLLM-compatible provider
- Integration/channel (if any): memory-lancedb plugin
- Relevant config (redacted): `embedding.model=text-embedding-3-small`, `embedding.dimensions=4`

### Steps

1. Configure `memory-lancedb` with an OpenAI-compatible provider that does not accept `dimensions`.
2. Trigger memory recall so an embedding request is created.
3. Observe that the request no longer includes `dimensions` and the returned vector is truncated locally.

### Expected

- The provider request succeeds without `dimensions`.
- The embedding is truncated locally to the configured dimension.
- If the provider returns too few dimensions, the plugin throws a clear error.

### Actual

- Before the fix, the request could fail because `dimensions` was forwarded upstream.
- After the fix, the request succeeds and truncation happens locally.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification log:

```text
$ pnpm test extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts -t "truncates OpenAI embeddings locally when dimensions are configured|fails when the embedding is shorter than the configured dimensions"

✓ extension-memory  extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts (21 tests | 19 skipped)
✓ 2 embedding regression tests passed
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local truncation when configured dimensions are present; clear failure when the returned vector is too short.
- Edge cases checked: missing upstream `dimensions`, short embedding rejection.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: a provider may return an embedding shorter than the requested local dimension.
  - Mitigation: the code now fails fast with a clear error instead of storing a malformed vector.

This PR strengthens the OpenRouter base URL normalization introduced in #68574 to handle case-insensitive URL matching and adds regression tests for edge cases.

target.title: docs(acp): clarify thread-bound follow-up orchestration
target.body:
## Summary
- clarify ACP thread-bound follow-up semantics
- document that thread continuity does not imply verbatim relay
- note that parent assistant should remain the default interpreter unless exact relay is explicitly requested

## Why
In thread-bound ACP workflows, users often send shorthand follow-ups like "continue with what you suggested" or "bắt nó làm tiếp như m suggest đi".

The parent assistant is expected to interpret those follow-ups and synthesize a concrete child prompt. Without this clarification, thread continuity can be misread as permission for raw pass-through, which degrades orchestration quality.

## Changes
- update `ACP_SPAWN_SESSION_ACCEPTED_NOTE` in `src/agents/acp-spawn.ts`
- add `docs/acp-followup-orchestration.md`

## Scope
This PR is a semantics/docs clarification pass only. It does not yet implement the deeper runtime behavior change in `sessions.send` / session continuation logic.

## Follow-up work
A future runtime patch should make parent-orchestrated follow-up rewriting the default for bound ACP sessions, with verbatim relay only when explicitly requested.

target.title: fix(bootstrap): recognize Bun error shape in isDirectModuleNotFoundError
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `bun openclaw.mjs` crashing on bootstrap when the optional `warning-filter` import is missing.

Bun does not set `err.url` on `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` errors and uses the original relative specifier in its error message instead of a resolved absolute path. The existing `isDirectModuleNotFoundError` helper relied on Node-specific properties (`err.url` and absolute path in `err.message`), causing the catch block to re-throw on Bun instead of swallowing the expected optional-import miss.

## Changes

- `openclaw.mjs`: add Bun-aware check for `err.specifier` (Bun's `ResolveMessage` exposes the original import specifier here)
- `openclaw.mjs`: add fallback message matching for relative specifiers so Bun's relative-path error messages are recognized

## Type of change

- [x] Bug fix

## Testing

- `pnpm format:check openclaw.mjs` — passed
- `pnpm lint:core openclaw.mjs` — 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `pnpm test src/infra/openclaw-root.test.ts` — 9 passed
- `pnpm test test/openclaw-launcher.e2e.test.ts` — 3 passed


## Related Issue

Fixes #69589

target.title: fix(ui): resolve stale gateway URL under nginx reverse-proxy multi-instance setups
target.body:
## Summary

- When multiple OpenClaw instances are served behind a single nginx domain with different path prefixes (e.g. `/a`, `/b`, `/c`), switching between instances keeps the WebSocket URL pinned to whichever instance was visited first. Refreshing or reopening the browser does not correct it.
- Root cause: `loadSettings()` falls back to the shared legacy localStorage key when the current path's scoped key is absent. The legacy key holds the first instance's `gatewayUrl`, and the existing equality check treats it as a user-override rather than a stale default.
- Adds `isStaleGatewayUrl()` to detect cached URLs that share the same host but differ in pathname — indicating they originated from a sibling reverse-proxy path rather than an intentional user choice. When detected, the page-derived default is used instead.

## Changes

- **`ui/src/ui/storage.ts`**: Added `isStaleGatewayUrl()` helper and updated `loadSettings()` gateway URL resolution to ignore stale cross-path cached values while preserving intentionally different (cross-host) user overrides.

## Test plan

- [ ] Configure nginx with two path-based reverse proxies (e.g. `/a` and `/b`) pointing to separate OpenClaw instances
- [ ] Visit `/a/chat`, verify WebSocket connects to the `/a` backend
- [ ] Switch to `/b/chat`, verify WebSocket connects to the `/b` backend (not `/a`)
- [ ] Switch back to `/a/chat`, verify WebSocket still connects to `/a`
- [ ] Manually enter a custom cross-host gateway URL, verify it is preserved and not overridden
- [ ] Single-instance (no basePath) setup still works as before

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(messages): add responseFooter and enrich /usage full
target.body:
## Summary

_AI-assisted PR. Prepared with AI assistance against a repo snapshot; targeted validation only. See Repro + Verification and Human Verification for exact coverage._

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: OpenClaw had a native `messages.responsePrefix`, but no matching native footer surface for the normal reply pipeline. At the same time, built-in `/usage full` did not surface cumulative context usage inline, there was no inherited config default for response-usage footers, and successful block-streaming flows could drop usage/footer output when final body payloads were intentionally suppressed.
- Why it matters: Users want one native, name-agnostic way to surface identity, model, usage, context, and cost metadata directly on every reply without manual prompt hacks, duplicate footer lines, or per-session setup drift.
- What changed: This PR adds `messages.responseFooter`, extends the shared response-template resolver for late-bound usage/context/cost/session values, enriches built-in `/usage full` with model/context/session/cost data, adds inherited `agents.defaults.responseUsage`, composes built-in usage with custom footers into a single footer block, and preserves footer/usage delivery when block streaming suppresses final body payloads.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add a new `/usage context` mode, does not change message-tool outbound sends or other non-reply egress paths, and does not introduce generic free-form interpolation of arbitrary session fields; supported placeholders remain an explicit allowlist.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #40215
- Closes #12578
- Related #24014
- Related #17832
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: `#24014` and `#17832` are currently closed upstream, so they are referenced as related context even though this PR implements the requested behavior in those threads. This PR intentionally does **not** claim `#39913`, because message-tool outbound sends remain out of scope.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Response metadata was split across two incomplete paths: prefix handling existed as an early-bound template surface, while usage data was appended later as a separate footer line after payload assembly. That meant there was no first-class footer stage, no inherited default for usage footers, and no reliable composition point once successful block streaming had already decided to drop final body payloads.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no seam coverage for “block streaming succeeded and final payloads are empty but usage/footer still needs to be delivered,” and no explicit composition rule for combining built-in `/usage` output with a configured custom footer.
- Contributing context (if known): Usage/context/cost/session values are late-bound and in some cases only trustworthy after the reply completes and session state is refreshed. Existing prefix-only handling was not designed for that later lifecycle stage.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-usage-line.footer.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-usage.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-reply-pipeline.test.ts`
  - `src/config/runtime-overrides.test.ts`
  - `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`
  - `src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `/usage full` includes model, context window usage, session key, and estimated cost when available
  - `agents.defaults.responseUsage` is inherited by sessions that do not override it
  - explicit `responseUsage: "off"` remains explicit even when a global default exists, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` clears the override and re-enables inheritance
  - static `messages.responseFooter` values fold with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line to avoid duplicate footer lines
  - successful block streaming still returns a trailing footer/usage payload when final body payloads are intentionally suppressed
  - response prefixes do not leak raw late-bound `{cost}` / `{usageLine}` / context placeholders before those values are known
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The risk is in formatting, inheritance, and reply-payload composition. Helper-level tests catch resolver and composition regressions, while reply-runner seam tests catch the payload-loss and block-streaming lifecycle failures without needing live channel E2E runs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing prefix-template coverage already exercised the shared resolver pattern; this PR extends that surface and adds footer- and usage-specific cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- New optional `messages.responseFooter` config for the normal reply pipeline.
- `messages.responsePrefix` and `messages.responseFooter` now share the same case-insensitive, allowlisted template resolver for placeholders such as `{model}`, `{provider}`, `{identityName}`, `{effort}`, `{input}`, `{output}`, `{total}`, `{cacheRead}`, `{cacheWrite}`, `{context}`, `{contextMax}`, `{contextPercent}`, `{cost}`, `{usageLine}`, and `{session}`.
- Built-in `/usage full` is enriched to include cumulative context usage, model label, session key, and estimated cost when available.
- New inherited config: `agents.defaults.responseUsage` (`off` | `tokens` | `full`). Sessions without an explicit override inherit this default.
- Session-level explicit `responseUsage: "off"` is preserved even when a global default exists. `PATCH responseUsage: null` still clears the explicit override and returns the session to inherited behavior.
- When both `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` are enabled:
  - static footers compose with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware custom footers suppress the separate built-in usage line
  - cost-only custom footers still compose with built-in usage unless `{usageLine}` is explicitly included
- In successful block-streaming flows, usage/footer text can still be emitted as a trailing payload even when the final body payload is intentionally suppressed.
- For streamed prefixes, unresolved late-bound usage/context/cost placeholders are blanked instead of leaking raw `{cost}`-style tokens.
- Added `docs/response-footer.md` documenting config, placeholders, composition rules, and examples.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [optional responsePrefix applied]
  -> [optional /usage line appended late]
  -> [block streaming may suppress final payloads]
  -> [custom footer unavailable natively, or usage/footer can be lost or duplicated]

After:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [early-bound prefix resolved]
  -> [late-bound usage/context/cost/session template context resolved]
  -> [built-in usage line formatted]
  -> [responseFooter composed with or against usage]
  -> [footer block appended to last text payload or emitted as trailing payload]
  -> [reply sent without duplicate footer lines]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node/TypeScript repo snapshot in an offline CAAS container
- Model/provider: mocked reply-runner and gateway paths with synthetic provider/model/usage metadata
- Integration/channel (if any): normal reply pipeline, successful block-streaming reply pipeline, gateway session patch path, and TUI `/usage` toggle path; no live external channel in this container
- Relevant config (redacted):

### Steps

1. Configure `agents.defaults.responseUsage` and optionally configure `messages.responseFooter`.
2. Trigger a normal reply, a successful block-streaming reply, and one or more `/usage` mode changes (`off`, `tokens`, `full`).
3. Inspect the final reply payloads and the effective session `responseUsage` state.

### Expected

- Sessions without an explicit override inherit `agents.defaults.responseUsage`.
- `/usage full` includes per-message tokens plus cumulative context, model, session, and estimated cost when available.
- Static custom footers fold with built-in usage into one footer block, while usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line.
- Successful block streaming does not drop footer/usage output just because the final body payload was suppressed.
- Prefixes do not leak raw late-bound placeholders before values are known.

### Actual

- In the covered paths, inherited usage defaults, explicit `off`, and clear-to-inherit (`null`) semantics behave as expected.
- `/usage full` is enriched with context/model/session/cost data in the covered reply paths.
- Footer composition follows the intended static-vs-usage-aware rules, and successful block streaming still returns footer/usage content as a trailing payload.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.

commands.review_summary:
The branch adds GitHub Actions `::add-mask::` calls after derived checkout auth headers in CI/Testbox workflows, while the current head also changes `CHANGELOG.md` and agent plugin-tool auth/runtime code.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level. Current main derives checkout `auth_header` values and uses them in Git authorization headers without registering those derived values with `add-mask`; I did not run a live token-leak workflow against main.

target.title: security(ci): mask derived base64 auth header in custom checkout blocks
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(): beta blocker - ` and link the matching `Beta blocker: - ` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation. - Problem: The inline checkout scripts in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` build a Basic-auth header as `base64("x-access-token:${GITHUB_TOKEN}")` but never register the derived value with the runner's log scrubber. GitHub Actions only auto-masks the raw `GITHUB_TOKEN`, not values derived from it. - Why it matters: If any downstream step runs under `set -x`, emits verbose `git` tracing, or fails while printing the fetch command, the base64 blob can surface in public CI logs and be trivially decoded back to a live installation token. - What changed: Added `echo "::add-mask::${auth_header}"` immediately after the header is constructed in every custom checkout block (11 jobs), so the runner redacts the value from all subsequent log output. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to job graph, matrices, timeouts, permissions, triggers, runners, or any non-checkout step. Behavior is identical; only the logging surface is hardened. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: The custom checkout scripts bypass `actions/checkout` and construct their own `AUTHORIZATION: basic ` header. The runner's automatic secret masking only covers the literal `GITHUB_TOKEN` string, so the base64-encoded derivative is unprotected in logs. - Missing detection / guardrail: No lint or workflow audit rule flags derived-secret values that are not passed through `::add-mask::` before use. - Contributing context (if known): Multiple jobs duplicate the same checkout block, so the gap is replicated across every lane that uses it. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (`security-scm-fast` job, zizmor audit step) - Scenario the test should lock in: Any base64-encoded auth header built inline must be passed to `::add-mask::` before the first step that could emit it to logs. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: zizmor already runs on changed workflow files in CI and is the canonical static auditor for this class of issue. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): `security-scm-fast` runs zizmor on changed workflows. - If no new test is added, why not: The change is a one-line log-scrubber registration with no runtime behavior to assert; static workflow audit is the correct layer. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes None. ## Diagram (if applicable) For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`. ```text Before: [build auth_header=base64(token)] -> [git fetch ... AUTHORIZATION: basic $auth_header] -> [on verbose/failure: header visible in logs] After: [build auth_header=base64(token)] -> [::add-mask:: auth_header] -> [git fetch ...] -> [runner scrubs value from all logs] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Risk — a base64-encoded GitHub token derivative could leak via verbose shell traces, git debug output, or error paths that echo the fetch command. Mitigation — register the derived header with the runner's log scrubber via `::add-mask::` in every job that constructs it, so any accidental emission is redacted before reaching the log stream. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Ubuntu 24.04 (GitHub-hosted / Blacksmith runners per existing `runs-on` matrix) - Runtime/container: default runner image, no container - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): `.github/workflows/ci.yml` ### Steps 1. Push the branch and open this PR so the `CI` workflow runs. 2. Open any job that uses the custom checkout block (e.g. `build-artifacts`, `checks-fast-core`). 3. Inspect the raw log of the `Checkout` step and confirm no base64 `AUTHORIZATION` value is emitted; the existing `zizmor` audit on changed workflows passes. ### Expected - All jobs pass with identical behavior to `main`; any log line that would have contained the base64 header shows `***` instead. ### Actual - Matches expected on the pushed branch. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Demonstration run on my fork showing that `::add-mask::` scrubs a base64 `x-access-token:` header from GitHub Actions logs (dummy value, no real secret involved): - Without mask: `header value is eC1hY2Nlc3MtdG9rZW46ZGVtby12YWx1ZS1ub3QtYS1zZWNyZXQ=` - After `::add-mask::`: `header value is ***` Full run: https://github.com/anurag-bg-neu/openclaw/actions/runs/24701668471/job/72245961669 ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Confirmed the `::add-mask::` line is present in every custom checkout block that constructs `auth_header` (11 occurrences), placed immediately after header construction and before the first `git` invocation. - Edge cases checked: No shell quoting or escaping change; `auth_header` is still expanded the same way in the `http.extraheader` config. Retry loop and checkout semantics unchanged. - What you did **not** verify: Runtime behavior on the Blacksmith self-hosted runner class — indistinguishable from GitHub-hosted since `::add-mask::` is a runner workflow command, not a runner-class feature. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: None beyond the zero-behavior-change surface; `::add-mask::` is a log-scrubber directive and does not alter execution. - Mitigation: Existing `security-scm-fast` zizmor audit continues to gate workflow changes.

target.title: feat(memory): add staggerMs option to dreaming cron schedule
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Multiple OpenClaw instances sharing the same dreaming cron expression fire dreaming sweeps simultaneously, causing redundant concurrent work, I/O spikes, and potential race conditions on shared storage.
- **Why it matters**: Operators in replicated/HA deployments have no built-in way to stagger dreaming execution across instances.
- **What changed**: Added optional `staggerMs` config field to dreaming schedule, wired through the existing cron stagger infrastructure. Affects `extensions/memory-core/openclaw.json`, `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.ts`, and `src/memory-host-sdk/dreaming.ts`.
- **What did NOT change**: Dreaming behavior when `staggerMs` is unset (falls back to cron service default). No changes to non-dreaming cron jobs, storage logic, or other plugin surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #69531

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — new feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config field `dreaming.staggerMs` in plugin config (`openclaw.json`), allowing operators to set a random stagger window (in ms) for the dreaming cron schedule.
- When unset, behavior is unchanged (cron service default stagger applies).

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A — config-only change, no UI or non-trivial logic flow.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime: Node 22+
- Integration: memory-core plugin

### Steps

1. Configure multiple OpenClaw instances with the same dreaming cron (e.g., `0 3 * * *`)
2. Set `dreaming.staggerMs: 300000` in one instance
3. Observe that the instance with staggerMs delays execution by a random offset within the window

### Expected

Instances with `staggerMs` configured fire at random offsets within the window instead of all at the exact cron time.

### Actual

target.title: fix: prevent defaultAccountId leak in cross-provider message sends
target.body:
## Summary

Two bug fixes bundled in this PR:

---

### Fix 1: #69525 — Cross-provider message send leaks source session accountId to target channel

When sending messages across providers (e.g. Telegram → Feishu) with `allowAcrossProviders: true`, the source session's `defaultAccountId` was incorrectly propagated to the target channel plugin, which has no account registered under that name.

**File:** `src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.ts`

```typescript
const explicitlyProvidedAccountId = readStringParam(params, "accountId");
const allowAcrossProviders = cfg.tools?.message?.crossContext?.allowAcrossProviders === true;
const isCrossProvider = Boolean(
  allowAcrossProviders &&
    input.toolContext?.currentChannelProvider &&
    input.toolContext.currentChannelProvider !== channel,
);
let accountId = explicitlyProvidedAccountId ?? (isCrossProvider ? undefined : input.defaultAccountId);
```

---

### Fix 2: #69482 — Telegram "Allow always" source field rejected by gateway

Telegram "Allow always" approval handler writes `source: "allow-always"`, but the gateway schema for `exec.approvals.set` didn't include this field, causing `openclaw approvals set --gateway` to reject entries with `unexpected property 'source'`.

**File:** `src/gateway/protocol/schema/exec-approvals.ts`

Added `source: Type.Optional(Type.String())` to `ExecApprovalsAllowlistEntrySchema`.

---

### Also included: #69475 (partial) — Skill description fixes

Updated SKILL.md `description` fields for `gog` and `session-logs` to include `Use when:` trigger conditions per the Anthropic skill spec.

**Files:** `skills/gog/SKILL.md`, `skills/session-logs/SKILL.md`

target.title: feat(heartbeat): support model fallbacks via {primary,fallbacks} (#69434)
target.body:
## Summary

- Accept `agents.*.heartbeat.model` as `{ primary, fallbacks }` in addition to the existing string form, mirroring `agents.defaults.model`. Closes #69434.
- Heartbeat ticks now fail over to the next fallback on retriable provider errors (rate-limit/429, overload/502/503, timeout) so primary-provider quota exhaustion no longer silently stalls the heartbeat loop for hours.
- String form stays fully backwards-compatible; new object form is additive.

## Design

- Schema: `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` reuses `AgentModelSchema` (the same union already used by `agents.defaults.model`, `imageModel`, etc.), and `src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts` replaces `model?: string` with `model?: AgentModelConfig`.
- Plumbing: `heartbeat-runner.ts` resolves primary + fallbacks via the shared `resolveAgentModelPrimaryValue` / `resolveAgentModelFallbackValues` helpers and passes them through a new `GetReplyOptions.heartbeatModelFallbacks` to the reply pipeline. `get-reply-run.ts` then stores them on a new `FollowupRun.run.modelFallbacksOverride` field. `resolveModelFallbackOptions` (used by both `agent-runner-execution.ts` and the memory flush path) and the inline fallback in `followup-runner.ts` prefer this override over `resolveRunModelFallbacksOverride`, so the existing `runWithModelFallback` machinery and its retriable-error classifier (429/502/503/timeout) transparently pick up the heartbeat chain.
- `get-reply.ts` switches the defaults-fallback read to `resolveAgentModelPrimaryValue(agentCfg?.heartbeat?.model)` so object-form defaults resolve correctly.
- `HeartbeatSummary` (consumed by `/status`, `/health`, compaction diagnostics, run attempts) keeps `model?: string` (now the primary) and gains an optional `modelFallbacks?: string[]` — no existing reader breaks.
- `gateway/model-pricing-cache.ts` migrates `heartbeat.model` registration from `addResolvedModelRef` to `addModelListLike`, so heartbeat fallbacks get the same pricing prefetch treatment as the agent-level model list.
- Empty `fallbacks: []` in the object form disables the fallback chain for that heartbeat without replacing it.

## Docs / config help

- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` documents both forms and the retriable-error failover semantics.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` + `src/config/schema.labels.ts` add help text and labels for `heartbeat.model`, `.primary`, and `.fallbacks` at both defaults and per-agent scopes.
- Changelog entry under `## Unreleased` → `### Changes`.

## Test plan

- [x] New unit tests in `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.model-override.test.ts`:
  - passes primary + fallbacks when `heartbeat.model` is an object
  - omits fallbacks when object form has no `fallbacks` array
  - propagates per-agent heartbeat fallbacks after merging defaults
- [x] 11 pre-existing heartbeat-model-override tests still pass (string form regression guard)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:prod` (core + extensions) green
- [x] `pnpm lint:core` 0/0
- [x] `pnpm format:check` clean
- [x] `pnpm test:changed` — 2248 tests passing across 246 files
- [x] `pnpm config:schema:gen` + `pnpm config:docs:gen` baselines regenerated and checked in
- [ ] Manual: verify a 429-exhausted primary provider actually fails over to a second provider in a live tick (covered indirectly through `runWithModelFallback` unit tests + `heartbeat-runner` tests; end-to-end live repro is in the reporter's production environment)

## Notes

- Pre-commit hook was bypassed via `FAST_COMMIT=1` (documented escape hatch) to avoid a pre-existing `src/entry.version-fast-path.test.ts:44` test-type failure on an untouched file. All other gates (format, lint, core typecheck, tests, doc baselines) pass.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix: resolve SecretRef keychain tokens in desktop app browser proxy
target.body:
## Problem

When `gateway.auth.token` is configured as a SecretRef (e.g., `{source: "exec", provider: "keychain", id: "gateway-auth-token"}`), the macOS desktop app fails to resolve the actual token value. This causes:

1. Desktop app reads `gateway.auth.token` as a dictionary, not a string
2. The `as? String` cast fails → token resolves to nil
3. Browser proxy requests to the gateway control server have no auth header
4. Gateway returns 401 Unauthorized
5. All browser tool calls via `node.invoke` fail with `UNAVAILABLE: Unauthorized`

## Fix

- Detect when `gateway.auth.token` or `gateway.auth.password` is a SecretRef dictionary with `provider: "keychain"`
- Resolve the actual value from the macOS keychain using `/usr/bin/security find-generic-password`
- Fall through to nil if resolution fails (preserving existing behavior)

## Testing

- Verified the bug locally: `security find-generic-password -s gateway-auth-token -w` returns empty (wrong lookup), while `-s openclaw -a gateway-auth-token -w` returns the actual token
- Confirmed browser control server accepts the token when passed via `Authorization: Bearer <token>`
- The fix resolves the token correctly using the `service + account` keychain lookup pattern

target.title: fix(runner): ancestry-aware cleanup of rewrite-abandoned transcript entries
target.body:
## Problem

Each call to `rewriteTranscriptEntriesInSessionFile` leaves the pre-rewrite
entries as abandoned sibling branches of the new leaf (append-only session
semantics: `branch(parent)` + re-append of the entire suffix). openclaw's
transcript reader scans every line in the file, so each successive rewrite
piles on another generation of the same turn.

This surfaced in live traces on the main agent session as **real
live-write duplication** after auto-compaction retries:

- The same 2312-byte user prompt persisted **12 times** in a single
  session file, with `parentId` chaining through successive compaction
  markers (one re-append per compaction attempt + a burst of extras).
- Assistant responses re-appended in the **same millisecond** after each
  `truncateOversizedToolResultsInSessionManager` call, because every tool-
  result truncation triggers a branch-and-reappend of the whole downstream
  suffix.
- Auto-compaction saw 169 → 91 → 92 → 93 → 94 messages across three
  attempts and still overflowed, because the re-appended generations were
  being counted alongside the live branch. The session was eventually
  restarted and the chain persisted into the successor, locking in the
  loop across session-ID boundaries.

Root cause lives in the rewrite path itself: the session DAG is
append-only, but the transcript reader treats the raw file as flat, so
abandoned branches show up as duplicates.

## Fix

After `rewriteTranscriptEntriesInSessionManager` returns `changed: true`,
reconcile the on-disk state with what was actually abandoned by this
specific rewrite — **and nothing else**:

1. Snapshot the active-branch ids via `sessionManager.getBranch()` BEFORE
   the rewrite. Sibling branches that already exist as alternate paths
   are not on this branch and are therefore out of scope by construction.
2. Run the rewrite.
3. Snapshot the active-branch ids AFTER the rewrite.
4. Candidate abandoned set = `branchIdsBefore \\ branchIdsAfter`.
5. **Ancestry-reference integrity filter:** walk `parentId` chains
   backward from every non-abandoned entry in the file and collect every
   id visited. Those ids are load-bearing even if they are candidates.
6. Final removable set = `candidates \\ ancestry-of(non-abandoned)`.
7. Drop only those ids, atomically via tmp-file + rename.

Identity is structural (id and `parentId` chain), not textual.

### Why the ancestry filter matters

A legitimate sibling branch can fork off an entry that the rewrite itself
replaces — the sibling's `parentId` still references the pre-rewrite id.
Without the ancestry filter the cleanup would remove that id, orphan the
sibling, break `SessionManager.getTree()`, and destroy shared-history
semantics. With the filter the shared-ancestor entry stays in the file
as long as any non-abandoned entry still references it.

This is consistent with the repo invariant already asserted at the
truncation boundary in
`src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/session-truncation.test.ts`
(\"preserves unsummarized sibling branches during truncation\"), now
extended to the rewrite boundary as well.

## Tests

New file: `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/transcript-rewrite.leaf-branch.test.ts`.

Four targeted tests on real temporary session files:

1. **Linear case** — single rewrite, no duplicate content for downstream
   messages (unique message ids, content counts exactly 1).
2. **Repeated rewrites** — two sequential rewrites on the same active
   path don't pile up cumulative duplicates of the same turn.
3. **Sibling before rewrite-target** — a legitimate sibling branch that
   forks before the rewrite target stays intact in
   `SessionManager.getEntries()` and the raw file.
4. **Sibling inside the rewritten suffix** — a sibling branch forks
   directly from the tool-result entry that the rewrite then replaces.
   Asserts not only entry existence but transitive `parentId` ancestry:
   every parent id in the sibling's chain must still resolve. Before the
   ancestry filter this failed with
   `sibling ancestry broken: parentId <X> of <Y> no longer present`.

All existing related tests continue to pass alongside the new ones:
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/session-truncation.test.ts` (preserves siblings)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/context-engine-maintenance.test.ts`
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/session-compaction-checkpoints.test.ts`

Local `pnpm check` (tsgo, lint, import-cycle, madge) green.

## Out of scope

Deliberately not touched in this PR:

- Reset/restore epoch semantics on the compaction chain (unrelated,
  tracked separately on PR #68765).
- The compaction-chain reader in `readSessionMessages` /
  `resolveMainSessionTranscriptFiles` (also PR #68765).
- Historical rewrite debris from sessions created before this fix;
  only new rewrites self-reconcile. Retroactive cleanup would be a
  separate change.

target.title: fix(msteams): paginate thread replies and keep recent context
target.body:
Title: fix(msteams): paginate thread replies and keep recent context

## Summary

- Problem: Teams thread context enrichment only used the first Graph replies page, so long threads could skew toward the oldest replies.
- Why it matters: the bot could miss the newest thread context, which is usually the most relevant context for a channel-thread reply.
- What changed: `fetchThreadReplies` now follows `@odata.nextLink` under a hard page cap and returns the most recent bounded reply window instead of only the first page.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no live-tenant Graph validation, no changes to higher-level message-handler behavior, and no prompt-formatting changes beyond the selected reply window.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the Teams thread helper fetched only the initial Graph replies page even when Graph exposed additional pages via `@odata.nextLink`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no unit coverage for paginated reply retrieval or for preferring the newest bounded reply window.
- Contributing context (if known): the Graph replies endpoint is oldest-first and does not support `$orderby`, so a single-page implementation silently favors stale context on long threads.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when Graph returns multiple reply pages for a channel thread, the helper follows pagination and keeps the most recent bounded reply window rather than only the first page.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is isolated to the Graph reply helper and can be reproduced deterministically with mocked paginated Graph responses.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this change.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Long Teams channel threads can now contribute newer reply context to the bot instead of only the oldest reply page.
- Behavior stays best-effort: if Graph pagination or fetches fail, the surrounding handler still degrades gracefully as before.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[long Teams thread] -> [fetch first replies page only] -> [oldest replies injected] -> [bot may miss latest context]

After:
[long Teams thread] -> [follow paginated replies with hard cap] -> [keep most recent bounded window] -> [bot sees newer thread context]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (Yes)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Risk: this can issue additional Graph requests for long threads.
  - Mitigation: pagination is capped at 10 pages and the helper still returns only the requested bounded reply window.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: repo-local Node via `pnpm openclaw`
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): MSTeams Graph helper unit tests
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Mock the first Graph replies page with `reply-1`, `reply-2`, and an `@odata.nextLink`.
2. Mock the second page with `reply-3`, `reply-4`.
3. Call `fetchThreadReplies(..., limit=2)`.

### Expected

- The helper should follow the next link and return the newest bounded window: `reply-3`, `reply-4`.

### Actual

- Before the fix, the helper would only use the first page and return `reply-1`, `reply-2`, dropping the newer page entirely.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: targeted unit run for `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.test.ts`; paginated reply path; hard page-cap path; existing thread-helper tests remained green.
- Edge cases checked: missing `value`, min/max limit clamping, bounded pagination cap.
- What you did **not** verify: live Microsoft Graph behavior against a real Teams tenant or real long thread pagination ordering/latency.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Microsoft Graph pagination behavior for long Teams threads has not been validated against a real tenant in this change.
  - Mitigation: the PR makes the assumption explicit, keeps the change local to the helper, and adds deterministic regression tests around the intended pagination behavior.
- Risk: extra Graph fetches could increase latency on large threads.
  - Mitigation: page following is hard-capped and still returns only a bounded recent window.

target.title: agents/context: unlock context1m on proxied Anthropic 1M-capable models (#69353)
target.body:
Fixes #69353.

### Summary
`isAnthropic1MModel` in `src/agents/context.ts` gates the 1M context window on `provider === "anthropic"`, so `context1m: true` was a silent no-op for any provider proxying Claude Opus 4 / Sonnet 4 — notably the bundled `github-copilot` provider, which routes to the same underlying Anthropic models but identifies itself as `"github-copilot"`.

This change keeps the prefix check (`claude-opus-4*`, `claude-sonnet-4*`) but drops the provider guard, so `context1m: true` works across proxying providers. The model-id prefix is still the authoritative filter, so non-Anthropic-capable models (e.g. `claude-haiku-3-5`) stay at their configured / fallback window.

### Why
Users configuring `agents.defaults.models["github-copilot/claude-opus-4-6"].params.context1m = true` observed sessions still compacting at ~180k tokens. The user opt-in is an explicit trust signal; the provider guard was over-restrictive.

### Implementation
- `src/agents/context.ts`: rename helper to `isAnthropic1MCapableModel(model)` and drop the unused `provider` parameter. The prefix filter (`ANTHROPIC_1M_MODEL_PREFIXES = ["claude-opus-4", "claude-sonnet-4"]`) remains the source of truth. Precedent: `extensions/anthropic/stream-wrappers.ts` already defines an identically-scoped `isAnthropic1MModel(modelId)` that only looks at the model id.
- `src/agents/context.test.ts`: two regression tests.
  1. `github-copilot` + `claude-opus-4-6` + `context1m: true` → returns 1M.
  2. `github-copilot` + `claude-haiku-3-5` + `context1m: true` → still returns 200k (prefix gate intact).

### Verification
- `pnpm test src/agents/context.test.ts` → 15/15 (13 pre-existing + 2 new).
- No unintentional behavior change for `provider === "anthropic"` callers; all prior assertions in `context.test.ts` still pass unchanged.
- Pre-existing `src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts` type errors on `origin/main` are unrelated (upstream commit 16985aba4e, not in this diff).

### Notes
- Backwards compatible. `context1m` remains an explicit opt-in, the prefix filter is unchanged, and the fallback paths (per-model `contextTokens`, cached/discovery windows) are untouched.
- AI-assisted contribution.

target.title: [Bug] Fix Telegram message-tool reply previews causing disappearing messages
target.body:
## Summary

Refs #80520.

This fixes one Telegram visible-reply path where ordinary turns configured for message-tool-only replies could still create Telegram draft/preview callbacks before the shared channel-message source-reply policy suppressed final source delivery.

- Resolve the source reply delivery mode with `resolveChannelMessageSourceReplyDeliveryMode` before enabling Telegram draft streaming.
- When that shared policy resolves to `message_tool_only`, skip Telegram partial/reasoning preview callbacks and pass `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"` into the reply dispatcher.
- Preserve automatic source delivery for authorized explicit commands, and keep unauthorized group slash text on the message-tool-only path.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram agent acknowledgement / preview messages could appear briefly and then disappear when visible replies were configured for `message_tool`. This matches the reporter repro in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80520#issuecomment-4510305367: `Send me 3 test messages` produced a disappearing acknowledgement while the three durable Telegram messages remained.

- **Real environment tested**: A real local OpenClaw install using the Telegram extension against an actual Telegram direct message chat with `@JIMCLAW_bot`, on May 22, 2026 in America/Chicago. The live setup had `messages.visibleReplies = "message_tool"` and `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies = "message_tool"`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Applied the patch to the local OpenClaw install, restarted the local OpenClaw gateway, then sent this real Telegram message to the bot: `Send 3 more test messages and and acknowledgement`. After that, checked the local OpenClaw runtime log around the 08:58 test window for inbound/outbound Telegram delivery entries and draft/delete cleanup entries.

- **Evidence after fix**: Reporter-provided after-fix Telegram screenshots show three durable bot replies at 8:58 AM with no disappearing acknowledgement bubble: `/home/jim/Downloads/Screenshot_20260522_085940_Telegram.jpg` and `/home/jim/Downloads/Screenshot_20260522_085944_Telegram.jpg`. Redacted runtime log excerpt from the patched live OpenClaw setup:

  ```text
  08:58:40.806 inbound telegram:<redacted-user-id> -> @JIMCLAW_bot direct message
  08:58:49.557 telegram outbound send ok messageId=814
  08:58:49.563 gateway/ws message.action success channel=telegram
  08:58:49.914 telegram outbound send ok messageId=815
  08:58:49.920 gateway/ws message.action success channel=telegram
  08:58:50.833 telegram outbound send ok messageId=816
  08:58:50.839 gateway/ws message.action success channel=telegram
  ```

  A log search around that proof window found no Telegram draft/delete cleanup entries for the successful patched test.

- **Observed result after fix**: Telegram displayed exactly three durable bot replies: `Test message 1 of 3 ✅`, `Test message 2 of 3 — sequential delivery confirmed`, and `Test message 3 of 3 — all three sent with acknowledgement`. The disappearing acknowledgement/preview behavior did not recur, and the reporter confirmed: “it looks like its working. No disappearing messages.”

- **What was not tested**: Full repository-wide build/check/test was not run locally because `pnpm` is not available on PATH on this machine. I did run the focused Telegram dispatcher Vitest file directly, plus the real Telegram live setup proof above.

## Related Issue Comments

- User repro comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80520#issuecomment-4510305367
- Related canonical no-send/drop evidence:
  - https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80520#issuecomment-4455610365
  - https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80520#issuecomment-4470879235
  - https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80520#issuecomment-4492527435
  - https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80520#issuecomment-4492534915

## Validation

- `git diff --check` passed.
- Focused Telegram extension Vitest passed:
  - `timeout 90s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
  - Result: 87 tests passed in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`.
- Real Telegram live install proof passed as described above.

## Not Run / Untested

- `pnpm test:extension telegram` was attempted but could not run on this machine because `pnpm` is not available on PATH (`pnpm: command not found`). I ran the underlying Vitest config directly instead.
- Full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` was not run for the same local tooling reason.
- `pnpm test:contracts` was not run.
