target.title: Policy: add agent workspace conformance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a policy-only conformance surface for locking agent workspace posture down to read-only/no-write configurations.

- records agent/default sandbox `workspaceAccess` and tool deny coverage as policy evidence
- adds `agents.workspace.allowedAccess` and `agents.workspace.denyTools` checks
- documents the supported read-only policy shape, evidence payload, and attestation-hash impact

This is stacked after #81981 and intentionally stays at config/policy conformance level; it does not add runtime enforcement.

## Policy syntax

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "workspace": {
      "allowedAccess": ["none", "ro"],
      "denyTools": ["exec", "process", "write", "edit", "apply_patch"],
    },
  },
}
```

Enabling or upgrading these rules adds `agentWorkspace` evidence to the workspace and attestation hashes. Operators should review the new evidence and refresh accepted attestation hashes after enabling these rules.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts --run --reporter=dot` (113 passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts --run --reporter=dot` (12 passed)
- `node node_modules/@typescript/native-preview/bin/tsgo.js -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-policy-agent-workspace-rebased.tsbuildinfo`
- `env PATH="/tmp/npm-11-6-2-bin:$PATH" node scripts/generate-npm-shrinkwrap.mjs --all --check` (matches CI npm 11.6.2)
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `/mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` (clean: no accepted/actionable findings)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Policy conformance can require agent sandbox workspace access to stay within an allowlist and require explicit deny coverage for shell/process/file-mutation tools.
Real environment tested: WSL checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-policy-agent-workspace` on Ubuntu-24.04.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/policy-proof-85096/openclaw.json timeout 180 node --import tsx -e "import('./src/cli/run-main.ts').then(m=>m.runCli(['node','openclaw','policy','check','--json','--severity-min','error']))"`
Evidence after fix: Malformed policy shape produced `policy/policy-jsonc-invalid`; nonconforming config produced `policy/agents-workspace-access-denied` and `policy/agents-tool-not-denied`; accepted read-only posture produced `ok: true` with zero findings.
Observed result after fix: The policy check emitted `agentWorkspace` evidence and 31 policy checks, including the new agent workspace checks.
What was not tested: No runtime sandbox/tool enforcement was tested because this PR only adds config-level policy conformance.

### Redacted CLI evidence

Malformed policy:

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "checksRun": 31,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "policy/policy-jsonc-invalid",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "policy.jsonc agents.workspace.allowedAccess must be an array.",
      "target": "oc://policy.jsonc/agents/workspace/allowedAccess"
    }
  ]
}
```

Nonconforming config:

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "checksRun": 31,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "policy/agents-workspace-access-denied",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "agents.defaults sandbox workspaceAccess 'rw' is not allowed by policy.",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/agents/defaults/sandbox/workspaceAccess",
      "requirement": "oc://policy.jsonc/agents/workspace/allowedAccess"
    },
    {
      "checkId": "policy/agents-tool-not-denied",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "agents.defaults does not deny required tool 'apply_patch'.",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/tools/deny",
      "requirement": "oc://policy.jsonc/agents/workspace/denyTools"
    }
  ]
}
```

Accepted read-only posture:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "checksRun": 31,
  "findings": [],
  "agentWorkspace": [
    {
      "id": "agents-defaults-workspace-access",
      "kind": "workspaceAccess",
      "source": "oc://openclaw.config/agents/defaults/sandbox/workspaceAccess",
      "scope": "defaults",
      "value": "ro",
      "explicit": true
    },
    {
      "id": "agents-defaults-tool-apply_patch",
      "kind": "toolDeny",
      "source": "oc://openclaw.config/tools/deny",
      "scope": "defaults",
      "tool": "apply_patch",
      "denied": true,
      "explicit": true
    }
  ]
}
```

target.title: feat(matrix): opt-in mention bypass for in-thread replies (threadBindings.bypassMentionInBoundThreads)
target.body:
Fixes #85111.

## Summary

Adds an opt-in `channels.matrix.threadBindings.bypassMentionInBoundThreads` (boolean, default false). When true, the matrix inbound handler skips the room-level `requireMention` gate for events that are replying inside a thread the bot already has prior activity in.

This enables the common bot UX of "mention to open a thread, free-form conversation continues inside it" on `requireMention` rooms — currently impossible because every in-thread reply that omits the mention is dropped as `skipping room message: no-mention`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: The matrix plugin's inbound handler drops in-thread replies in `requireMention: true` rooms even when the bot has been actively conversing in that thread. Users have to `@bot` on every line inside threads the bot opened, breaking the natural "mention to start, free-form continuation" UX. With this PR's opt-in flag, in-thread replies bypass the mention gate when the bot has a recorded session for the thread (via `recordInboundSession`'s thread-suffixed key) OR an explicit runtime binding (via `/focus`, `/acp bind`, etc.). Top-level messages keep `requireMention` enforcement; rooms without the flag set behave byte-identically to current `main`.

- **Real environment tested**: Unraid 7.2.5 host, `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest` (2026.5.19), Synapse via `ghcr.io/junkerderprovinz/matrix:latest` (AIO image), both containers on the same Docker bridge, Synapse Tailscale-served as `matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net`. Test room `parents` (`!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net`) configured under `channels.matrix.groups[<roomId>] = { requireMention: true, skills: [actual-budget, diagram-maker, news-summary] }`, three members (`@paulmeier`, `@openclaw`, `@admin`), E2EE on, `threadReplies: \"always\"`, `threadBindings: { enabled: true, idleHours: 24, spawnSessions: true, defaultSpawnContext: \"fork\" }`. The patched matrix monitor bundle (`/root/.openclaw/extensions/matrix/dist/monitor-4YYdKe_C.js`) was overlaid onto the running container via `docker cp`, then OpenClaw restarted. The unpatched bundle was preserved at `monitor-4YYdKe_C.js.bak` for revert.

- **Exact steps or command run after the patch**: Live-environment verification — overlay the patched matrix monitor bundle onto the running container, restart, then exercise the in-thread reply path in `#parents`:

  ```
  $ ssh root@<unraid> 'docker cp OpenClaw:/root/.openclaw/extensions/matrix/dist/monitor-4YYdKe_C.js /tmp/monitor-orig.js'
  $ scp root@<unraid>:/tmp/monitor-orig.js /tmp/monitor-orig.js
  $ python3 -c \"src=open('/tmp/monitor-orig.js').read();
    old='const shouldBypassMention = allowTextCommands && isRoom && shouldRequireMention && !wasMentioned && !hasExplicitMention && commandAuthorized && hasControlCommandInMessage;';
    new='const inThreadReply = threadRootId !== undefined && threadRootId !== messageId; let threadSessionExists = false; if (inThreadReply) { try { const _storePath = core.channel.session.resolveStorePath(cfg.session?.store, { agentId: _route.agentId }); const _ts = core.channel.session.readSessionUpdatedAt({ storePath: _storePath, sessionKey: _route.sessionKey }); threadSessionExists = _ts !== undefined && _ts !== null; } catch (_e) { threadSessionExists = false; } } const inExistingBoundThread = inThreadReply && (threadSessionExists || _runtimeBindingId !== null); const shouldBypassMention = isRoom && shouldRequireMention && !wasMentioned && !hasExplicitMention && ((allowTextCommands && commandAuthorized && hasControlCommandInMessage) || inExistingBoundThread);';
    open('/tmp/monitor-patched.js','w').write(src.replace(old, new, 1))\"
  $ scp /tmp/monitor-patched.js root@<unraid>:/tmp/
  $ ssh root@<unraid> 'docker cp /tmp/monitor-patched.js OpenClaw:/root/.openclaw/extensions/matrix/dist/monitor-4YYdKe_C.js && docker restart OpenClaw'
  ```

  In `#parents`, posted `@openclaw ping` (top-level mention; opens a thread), then in that thread posted `hello there` (no mention).

  Runtime acceptance-criteria suite — runtime executed locally against the patched branch (`308da8c5`):

  ```
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/direct-room.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/direct-management.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/direct.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/send/targets.test.ts
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Live patched-runtime gateway log on the deployment, redacted — bot processing the in-thread reply that was previously dropped. After the user's mention of \`@openclaw\` opens the thread, the in-thread \`hello there\` follow-up triggers an outbound encrypted-message burst (Olm session prepares for the typing indicator + final reply) in the parents room. No \`skipping room message\` entries fire for the in-thread events:

  ```
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:43:08.376-05:00\" \"matrix: logged in as @openclaw:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.376-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648376.1: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.464-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648464.2: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.537-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648537.3: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.614-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648614.4: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
   (+ 7 more bursts of streaming-quiet typing updates ending at m1779446649228.11)
  ```

  11 outbound encrypted-message preparations in the same thread within 853 ms — the streaming-quiet typing-indicator pattern fires throughout the agent turn, then a final reply. Compare to the pre-patch deployment captured earlier in #85111's reproduction, where the same in-thread message produced a `\"message\":\"skipping room message\"` entry at `handler.ts:1032` with no outbound activity.

  Runtime acceptance suite (verbose) against `308da8c5` — the three behaviors this PR is responsible for:

  ```
   ✓ monitor/handler > createMatrixRoomMessageHandler > bypasses requireMention for in-thread replies when a session record already exists for the thread
   ✓ monitor/handler > createMatrixRoomMessageHandler > bypasses requireMention for in-thread replies when an explicit runtime binding exists (e.g. /focus)
   ✓ monitor/handler > createMatrixRoomMessageHandler > still drops in-thread replies without mention when bypassMentionInBoundThreads is off (default)
     (+ 107 other existing handler behaviors, all green)

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  110 passed (110)
     Duration  2.92s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the live patched deployment, an in-thread `hello there` (no `@openclaw` mention) inside the thread the bot just opened triggers the agent — Element shows `OpenClaw is typing…`, the user's message gets the `👀` ack reaction, the bot replies inside the same thread. Pre-patch, the identical message was logged as `skipping room message` and never reached the agent. Top-level non-mention messages in the same room continue to be dropped (legacy `requireMention` behavior preserved). With the new flag off (the default for everyone who doesn't opt in), behavior is byte-identical to current `main`, and the existing regression at `handler.test.ts:1617` (`does not refresh bound Matrix thread bindings for room messages dropped before routing`) continues to pass without modification.

- **What was not tested**: The unmodified-image runtime (i.e. with `bypassMentionInBoundThreads: true` set in `openclaw.json` and a CI-rebuilt `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest` carrying the patch through the official build pipeline). The live proof above uses an in-place bundle overlay on a running container, which exercises the patched mention-gate code path correctly but uses the always-on form of the discriminator (the running runtime has no way to read the new opt-in config field). The flag-gated form is what the source PR ships; the gate evaluation is covered by the three handler tests above, two of which exercise the bypass path and one of which exercises the legacy-default-off path. Maintainer-side CI rebuild will produce the final runtime artifact that exercises the flag-gated form end-to-end.

## Discriminator update vs the originally-pushed version

The first commit on this branch (`6f2a5b6e`) gated the bypass on `_runtimeBindingId !== null` only. Live testing surfaced that natural per-thread continuation does NOT produce a runtime binding — `sessionBindingService.resolveByConversation` returns `null` for threads that were opened by the bot replying, only for threads that have an explicit ACP/user-managed binding (via `/focus`, `/acp bind`, etc.). The handler tests in `6f2a5b6e` mocked `resolveByConversation` to return a binding, which masked the wrong discriminator: tests passed because the mock matched the broken code path, not because the real-world code path worked.

The current commit (`308da8c5`) replaces the single-signal check with an OR over two signals:

1. **Session-store record exists for the thread** — via `core.channel.session.readSessionUpdatedAt({ storePath, sessionKey: _route.sessionKey })`. The thread session key is set by `resolveMatrixInboundRoute` whenever `thread.threadId` is populated (always under `threadReplies: \"inbound\"`/`\"always\"`), and `recordInboundSession` persists it on the first message the bot processes in the thread. So this is the natural-continuation path.
2. **Explicit runtime binding exists** — the original `_runtimeBindingId !== null` check, kept as a parallel signal for the `/focus`-bound case.

The session-store path is the dominant case in real deployments; the runtime-binding path covers explicit-binding flows for parity. Either signal is sufficient.

## Files changed

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `extensions/matrix/src/config-schema.ts` | New `bypassMentionInBoundThreads` field in `matrixThreadBindingsSchema` |
| `extensions/matrix/src/types.ts` | Same field added to `MatrixThreadBindingsConfig` TS type |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/index.ts` | Read `accountConfig.threadBindings?.bypassMentionInBoundThreads === true` and pass into handler params |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.ts` | Handler params accept the flag; in-thread-reply discriminator computes session-store existence via `readSessionUpdatedAt`; OR with `_runtimeBindingId` and feeds `shouldBypassMention` |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts` | Three tests: session-record bypass, runtime-binding bypass, legacy default-off drop |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test-helpers.ts` | Test harness option `bypassMentionInBoundThreads` |
| `docs/channels/matrix.md` | Documents the new field plus discriminators and the preserved legacy contract |

## Related

- #85111 — the filed issue with full reproduction.

commands.author: valkyriweb

target.title: fix(agents): lock transcript-writing session events [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: embedded prompt-lock fencing can report a takeover after a successful visible reply when `message`/`custom_message` session events write transcript entries while the prompt lock is released.
- Why it matters: the user can receive the correct channel reply and then see a misleading generic failure trailer from the losing lane.
- What changed: the real Pi `_handleAgentEvent` listener path now acquires the embedded session write lock for events that can write transcript entries; `_processAgentEvent` remains a fallback seam.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no channel routing changes, no lane ownership changes, no changelog edit.

AI-assisted: yes. Prompt/session reference: Pi goal session `2026-05-22T13-29-51Z`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a direct channel image turn produced the correct visible reply, then a second generic failure trailer because the embedded runner detected session file drift after the prompt lock was released.
- Real environment observed: self-hosted OpenClaw gateway using a direct WhatsApp image message.
- Evidence before fix (redacted copied runtime log):

```text
[diagnostic] lane task error: lane=main durationMs=22823 error="EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: .../sessions/<session>.jsonl"
[diagnostic] lane task error: lane=session:agent:main:whatsapp:direct:+REDACTED durationMs=22833 error="EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: .../sessions/<session>.jsonl"
Embedded agent failed before reply: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: .../sessions/<session>.jsonl
```

Visible channel behavior before fix:

```text
<correct image description reply>
⚠️ Something went wrong while processing your request. Please try again, or use /new to start a fresh session.
```

- Observed cause: Pi agent events can write transcript entries through `_handleAgentEvent` while the embedded prompt lock is released, but the OpenClaw write-lock wrapper did not cover the real subscribed listener for all transcript-writing event types.
- Expected result after fix: visible reply events that can write the transcript are serialized through the same session write lock, so the embedded fence does not mistake local transcript writes for an external takeover.
- What was not tested: a full live WhatsApp replay after this exact upstream branch; the patch is verified with focused coverage that the final subscribed Pi listener is the locked `_handleAgentEvent` wrapper and that it awaits the Pi event queue while holding the lock.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the embedded attempt session lock released around prompt execution, but the real Pi `_handleAgentEvent` listener could process transcript-writing events outside the OpenClaw session write lock.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered a fabricated processing seam and did not prove the subscribed Pi listener was reconnected to the final locked handler.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `custom_message` and `message` events acquire the session write lock, the real `_handleAgentEvent` seam is wrapped, and the final reconnected listener captures the locked handler rather than the pre-lock awaitable wrapper.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the failure was caused by transcript-writing Pi event handling escaping the embedded session write lock.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing predicate coverage for other transcript-writing events.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Successful direct-channel replies should no longer be followed by a misleading generic failure trailer caused by local transcript event writes racing the embedded session fence.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
message event -> transcript write while prompt lock released -> session fingerprint drift -> takeover error -> failure trailer

After:
message event -> session write lock -> serialized transcript write -> no false takeover from local reply event
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

Commands run locally on this branch:

```bash
LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 LANG=en_US.UTF-8 pnpm --dir /private/tmp/openclaw-pr3-session-event-lock build
LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 LANG=en_US.UTF-8 pnpm --dir /private/tmp/openclaw-pr3-session-event-lock check
LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 LANG=en_US.UTF-8 pnpm --dir /private/tmp/openclaw-pr3-session-event-lock exec vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts
LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 LANG=en_US.UTF-8 pnpm --dir /private/tmp/openclaw-pr3-session-event-lock test
```

Results:

- `pnpm build`: passed.
- `pnpm check`: passed after the corrected `_handleAgentEvent` patch.
- Focused `attempt.session-lock.test.ts`: passed, including the `message` / `custom_message` lock coverage, real `_handleAgentEvent` coverage, queue-await coverage, and captured-listener reconnect regression.
- Full `pnpm test`: patch-area suites passed; unrelated pre-existing channel-suite failures were observed in `configured-state.test.ts` channel order and `qa-channel` registry contract metadata.

Local Codex review:

```bash
codex review --base upstream/main
```

## Reviewer Notes

- The fix intentionally stays at the session event write-lock seam instead of changing lane dispatch ownership.
- Non-transcript events without extension handlers remain unlocked.

target.title: Policy: add agent workspace conformance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a policy-only conformance surface for locking agent workspace posture down to read-only/no-write configurations.

- records agent/default sandbox `workspaceAccess` and tool deny coverage as policy evidence
- adds `agents.workspace.allowedAccess` and `agents.workspace.denyTools` checks
- documents the supported read-only policy shape, evidence payload, and attestation-hash impact

This is stacked after #81981 and intentionally stays at config/policy conformance level; it does not add runtime enforcement.

## Policy syntax

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "workspace": {
      "allowedAccess": ["none", "ro"],
      "denyTools": ["exec", "process", "write", "edit", "apply_patch"],
    },
  },
}
```

Enabling or upgrading these rules adds `agentWorkspace` evidence to the workspace and attestation hashes. Operators should review the new evidence and refresh accepted attestation hashes after enabling these rules.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts --run --reporter=dot` (113 passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts --run --reporter=dot` (12 passed)
- `node node_modules/@typescript/native-preview/bin/tsgo.js -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-policy-agent-workspace-rebased.tsbuildinfo`
- `env PATH="/tmp/npm-11-6-2-bin:$PATH" node scripts/generate-npm-shrinkwrap.mjs --all --check` (matches CI npm 11.6.2)
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `/mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` (clean: no accepted/actionable findings)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Policy conformance can require agent sandbox workspace access to stay within an allowlist and require explicit deny coverage for shell/process/file-mutation tools.
Real environment tested: WSL checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-policy-agent-workspace` on Ubuntu-24.04.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/policy-proof-85096/openclaw.json timeout 180 node --import tsx -e "import('./src/cli/run-main.ts').then(m=>m.runCli(['node','openclaw','policy','check','--json','--severity-min','error']))"`
Evidence after fix: Malformed policy shape produced `policy/policy-jsonc-invalid`; nonconforming config produced `policy/agents-workspace-access-denied` and `policy/agents-tool-not-denied`; accepted read-only posture produced `ok: true` with zero findings.
Observed result after fix: The policy check emitted `agentWorkspace` evidence and 31 policy checks, including the new agent workspace checks.
What was not tested: No runtime sandbox/tool enforcement was tested because this PR only adds config-level policy conformance.

### Redacted CLI evidence

Malformed policy:

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "checksRun": 31,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "policy/policy-jsonc-invalid",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "policy.jsonc agents.workspace.allowedAccess must be an array.",
      "target": "oc://policy.jsonc/agents/workspace/allowedAccess"
    }
  ]
}
```

Nonconforming config:

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "checksRun": 31,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "policy/agents-workspace-access-denied",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "agents.defaults sandbox workspaceAccess 'rw' is not allowed by policy.",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/agents/defaults/sandbox/workspaceAccess",
      "requirement": "oc://policy.jsonc/agents/workspace/allowedAccess"
    },
    {
      "checkId": "policy/agents-tool-not-denied",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "agents.defaults does not deny required tool 'apply_patch'.",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/tools/deny",
      "requirement": "oc://policy.jsonc/agents/workspace/denyTools"
    }
  ]
}
```

Accepted read-only posture:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "checksRun": 31,
  "findings": [],
  "agentWorkspace": [
    {
      "id": "agents-defaults-workspace-access",
      "kind": "workspaceAccess",
      "source": "oc://openclaw.config/agents/defaults/sandbox/workspaceAccess",
      "scope": "defaults",
      "value": "ro",
      "explicit": true
    },
    {
      "id": "agents-defaults-tool-apply_patch",
      "kind": "toolDeny",
      "source": "oc://openclaw.config/tools/deny",
      "scope": "defaults",
      "tool": "apply_patch",
      "denied": true,
      "explicit": true
    }
  ]
}
```

target.title: Preserve Codex and Telegram context evidence
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram prompt context cached inbound messages but not OpenClaw's own outbound Telegram replies, so a later context window could include Keshav's 01:37 request while omitting the adjacent 01:39 assistant answer.
- Problem: normal streamed Telegram assistant finals are finalized through the draft-stream/edit path, so caching only `sendMessageTelegram` sends still missed the central streamed-final path.
- Problem: Codex per-turn context-engine projections were flattening prior tool calls/results to placeholders, hiding evidence needed to answer follow-up debugging questions.
- Solution: cache both successful Telegram outbound sends and preview-finalized streamed finals into the Telegram message-context cache, and preserve sanitized tool evidence in active context-engine projections.
- Scope boundary: this does not make every old transcript globally searchable; it fixes the observed Telegram outbound-context gap and selected-context payload elision only.

## Motivation

Keshav pointed out that a later Codex turn did not show the earlier timeout-halving work or the 01:39 final answer evidence. Rechecking the actual 10:11 session showed no user `/new` or `/reset`: the Telegram selected context included the 01:37 user request but omitted OpenClaw's own 01:39 Telegram reply. A separate later prompt also showed `tool call: bash [input omitted]` and `tool result: ... [content omitted]`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #85219
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram outbound replies now survive into later Telegram prompt-context windows; Codex context-engine per-turn projections no longer reduce prior tool work to empty placeholders.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on this host, branch `fix/codex-context-tool-payloads`, head commit `8e3ea2be9e3462d3d60da14cf2290ccc796e2dc7`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran targeted Vitest coverage from the repo root. Also checked the production session logs that motivated the fix to confirm the before-state: 10:11 selected Telegram context contained Keshav's 01:37 request but omitted Kelaw's 01:39 reply; 10:18 Codex context contained only `[input omitted]` / `[content omitted]` tool placeholders.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Candidate runtime proof attached in [comment 4519833550](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85231#issuecomment-4519833550). The proof harness was temporary and removed after the run, leaving the PR diff unchanged.

  Candidate proof command:

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/manual/pr-85231-candidate-proof.test.ts --reporter verbose
```

  Candidate proof result:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       1 passed (1)
```

  Existing targeted regression run from the patched checkout:

```text
Test Files 4 passed (4)
Tests 218 passed (218)
```

- Observed result after fix: The candidate runtime proof invokes the production Telegram outbound-cache helper, reloads the persisted Telegram message cache, and shows the later selected prompt context includes the assistant outbound reply body. It also invokes the Codex context projection with `toolPayloadMode: "preserve"` and shows useful result text like `timeoutSeconds=7200` is retained while the raw shell command and fake API key are absent. The targeted regressions additionally cover normal sends, streamed-final preview finalization, and the run-attempt projection callsite.
- What was not tested: A full deployed Telegram-to-Codex production turn was not exercised. The current Mantis live Telegram catalog does not appear to include a scenario for internal prompt-context retention, so running a generic status/command scenario would not prove this PR's behavior. The attached candidate proof is the smallest available behavior proof without deploying this branch.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The captured 10:11 local Codex session prompt selected Keshav's 01:37 “halve those” message but not OpenClaw's 01:39 final answer. The captured 10:18 local Codex session prompt showed `tool call: bash [input omitted]` and `tool result: call_... [content omitted]`.

- Production Telegram media proof: GitHub-hosted screenshot of the reported 01:37 -> 01:39 -> 10:11 Telegram thread sequence.

  ![Telegram transcript showing the timeout-halving request, Kelaw confirmation, and later follow-up](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/keshavbotagent/openclaw/proof-pr-85231-telegram-screenshot/proof/pr-85231/telegram-context-proof.jpg)

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Telegram only recorded inbound messages in `message-cache.ts`; successful outbound sends were recorded for bot ownership/reactions but not for future prompt-context selection.
- Root cause: the first Telegram fix covered `sendMessageTelegram` sends but not preview-finalized streamed finals, which are delivered through draft-stream plus edit-message finalization.
- Root cause: `run-attempt.ts` passed `toolPayloadMode: contextEngineProjection ? "preserve" : "elide"`, so active per-turn context-engine projections used elision even when the projection engine had safe redaction support.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No regression covered Telegram outbound replies being present in later selected conversation context, streamed finals being written to the same cache, or active per-turn context-engine projections preserving redacted tool-result evidence.

## Prompt Boundary Review

ClawSweeper flagged the prompt-boundary expansion in `run-attempt.ts`. I reviewed the projection path and kept the current implementation because the fix needs prior tool-result evidence, not only tool metadata.

Boundary details:

- Tool-call inputs are not preserved as values; only the input shape and primitive types are rendered, so shell commands such as `cat .env` are omitted.
- Tool-result values are passed through `redactToolPayloadText` plus `redactSensitiveFieldValue`, which forces tool-mode redaction and applies built-in/user `logging.redactPatterns`.
- Rendered context is still selected by the context engine and capped by the projection budget; this PR does not add global transcript search.
- The remaining risk is intentional: non-secret private text in selected tool output may become model context. Narrowing that away would also remove the concrete evidence needed to avoid repeated work.

If maintainers do not want that boundary, the smallest alternative is to split this PR and merge only the Telegram outbound-cache fix while leaving Codex tool-result evidence elided.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`, `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`, `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: successful Telegram sends and preview-finalized streamed finals are recorded into the persisted prompt-context cache; active context-engine projection preserves useful tool-result evidence while redacting raw command/secret values.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it covers both Telegram delivery paths that can produce visible assistant replies and the Codex prompt sent to `turn/start`.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `context-engine-projection.test.ts` covers projection behavior directly; this PR adds callsite and Telegram outbound-cache coverage.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Future Telegram turns should be able to see OpenClaw's own recent Telegram replies in selected chat context, including streamed assistant finals, so a follow-up should not see only the user's earlier request without the bot answer. Codex should also have enough redacted prior tool-result context to avoid repeating completed work when that evidence exists in selected context.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/manual/pr-85231-candidate-proof.test.ts --reporter verbose` (temporary candidate proof harness, removed after run)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/message-cache.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

target.title: perf(cli): skip config load for default root help
target.body:
## Summary
- avoid importing the full config/validation stack for `openclaw --help` when there is no config file, no OpenClaw dotenv file, and no plugin-help-affecting env override
- keep the existing live-config path for configured/plugin-sensitive help, invalid config handling, and injected-env tests
- include the legacy `~/.config/openclaw/gateway.env` runtime dotenv compatibility path in the fast-path precheck unless an explicit non-default `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` is active

## Why
The root help fast path has precomputed help text, but it still called `readConfigFileSnapshot()` first to see whether plugin config could affect help output. In a default/no-config invocation that dragged in config validation before printing static help.

## Real behavior proof
behavior: default `openclaw --help` startup no longer imports the full config/validation stack when no config, dotenv, or plugin-help-affecting env can change static root help output. Plugin-sensitive help still uses the live config path, including the legacy `~/.config/openclaw/gateway.env` compatibility file unless an explicit non-default `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` is active.

environment: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, CLI startup artifacts built with `node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`, isolated temporary HOME/state directories, real CLI startup measured with `node scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case help`.

steps:
1. Built CLI startup artifacts: `node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`.
2. Measured the previous root help path with isolated temp-home runs: `node scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case help --runs 3 --warmup 0 --timeout-ms 10000 --json`.
3. Measured this PR's root help path with isolated temp-home runs: `node scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case help --runs 5 --warmup 0 --timeout-ms 10000 --json`.

target.title: fix(media): resolve media:// claim-check refs in loadImageFromRef and image-tool (#74123) [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `media://inbound/...` claim-check image refs were detected but then treated like workspace-relative paths in two native image-loading seams.
- Why it matters: agents could not recover an inbound image once a prompt or tool call carried the claim-check URI instead of a local file path.
- What changed: `loadImageFromRef` and the `image` tool now resolve inbound `media://` refs to their local media buffer before workspace path resolution.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no remote URL policy changes; no changelog edit.

AI-assisted: yes. Prompt/session reference: Pi goal session `2026-05-22T13-29-51Z`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #74123
- Related #74186
- Related #74278
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: when inbound images reached the native runner as `media://inbound/...` claim-check refs, image-loading seams resolved them as paths like `<workspace>/media:/inbound/...` and failed to load them.
- Real environment tested: self-hosted OpenClaw gateway using a direct channel image message.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: deployed the resolver wiring with the catalog fix, sent an image to the agent, then inspected the submitted provider trajectory and the visible channel reply.
- Evidence after fix (redacted copied runtime log):

```json
{
  "type": "prompt.submitted",
  "provider": "openai-codex",
  "modelId": "gpt-5.5",
  "data": {
    "imagesCount": 1,
    "msgContentTypes": ["image", "text"]
  }
}
```

Visible reply after fix:

```text
It’s a tan/cognac leather briefcase or laptop bag — structured rectangular shape, twin handles, detachable shoulder strap, zip top. The logo says “FOM” / “Freedom of Movement”.
```

- Observed result after fix: the agent could read and describe the inbound image.
- What was not tested: full channel E2E against every sandbox backend; focused sandbox bridge coverage now verifies native `media://` prompt-image loading.
- Before evidence (redacted copied runtime log):

```json
{
  "type": "prompt.submitted",
  "provider": "openai-codex",
  "modelId": "gpt-5.5",
  "data": {
    "prompt": "[media attached: media://inbound/<redacted>.jpg (image/jpeg)] ...",
    "imagesCount": 0
  }
}
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `media://inbound/...` refs were recognized as image refs, but these two consumers did not resolve the claim-check URI before generic workspace path resolution.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered detection and other seams, but not native `loadImageFromRef` or `image` tool resolution of `media://` refs.
- Contributing context (if known): this complements #74186 and #74278 by wiring two additional seams from #74123.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`, `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `media://inbound/...` refs are resolved through the media store before workspace path fallback, including native prompt-image loading with sandbox reads enabled.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: both failures were seam-local URI resolution gaps.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for these two seams.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Agents can recover inbound media claim-check image refs in native prompt image loading and the `image` tool instead of failing on a mangled workspace path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
media://inbound/id.jpg -> workspace path fallback -> <workspace>/media:/inbound/id.jpg -> ENOENT

After:
media://inbound/id.jpg -> media store resolver -> local media buffer path -> image loads
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux container deployment
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw gateway
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`
- Integration/channel (if any): direct channel image message
- Relevant config (redacted): standard inbound media storage with claim-check `media://inbound/...` refs

### Steps

1. Produce an inbound image ref as `media://inbound/<id>.jpg`.
2. Let the native image loader or `image` tool consume that ref.
3. Confirm the ref resolves through the media store instead of workspace path fallback.

### Expected

- The image loader receives a local media buffer path and loads the image.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the URI was resolved as a workspace-relative path containing `media:/inbound/...` and failed to load.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: live direct-channel image turn reached the model as `imagesCount: 1`; the model replied with a correct visual description.
- Edge cases checked: native prompt image loading, native prompt image loading with sandbox reads enabled, and `image` tool path resolution each have focused regression coverage.
- What you did **not** verify: full channel E2E against every sandbox backend.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: malformed or missing `media://` refs could be mistaken for file paths.
  - Mitigation: unresolved media refs stay on the existing failure path rather than being silently treated as valid files.

target.title: Policy: add agent workspace conformance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a policy-only conformance surface for locking agent workspace posture down to read-only/no-write configurations.

- records agent/default sandbox `workspaceAccess` and tool deny coverage as policy evidence
- adds `agents.workspace.allowedAccess` and `agents.workspace.denyTools` checks
- documents the supported read-only policy shape, evidence payload, and attestation-hash impact

This is stacked after #81981 and intentionally stays at config/policy conformance level; it does not add runtime enforcement.

## Policy syntax

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "workspace": {
      "allowedAccess": ["none", "ro"],
      "denyTools": ["exec", "process", "write", "edit", "apply_patch"],
    },
  },
}
```

Enabling or upgrading these rules adds `agentWorkspace` evidence to the workspace and attestation hashes. Operators should review the new evidence and refresh accepted attestation hashes after enabling these rules.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts --run --reporter=dot` (113 passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts --run --reporter=dot` (12 passed)
- `node node_modules/@typescript/native-preview/bin/tsgo.js -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-policy-agent-workspace-rebased.tsbuildinfo`
- `env PATH="/tmp/npm-11-6-2-bin:$PATH" node scripts/generate-npm-shrinkwrap.mjs --all --check` (matches CI npm 11.6.2)
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `/mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` (clean: no accepted/actionable findings)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Policy conformance can require agent sandbox workspace access to stay within an allowlist and require explicit deny coverage for shell/process/file-mutation tools.
Real environment tested: WSL checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-policy-agent-workspace` on Ubuntu-24.04.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/policy-proof-85096/openclaw.json timeout 180 node --import tsx -e "import('./src/cli/run-main.ts').then(m=>m.runCli(['node','openclaw','policy','check','--json','--severity-min','error']))"`
Evidence after fix: Malformed policy shape produced `policy/policy-jsonc-invalid`; nonconforming config produced `policy/agents-workspace-access-denied` and `policy/agents-tool-not-denied`; accepted read-only posture produced `ok: true` with zero findings.
Observed result after fix: The policy check emitted `agentWorkspace` evidence and 31 policy checks, including the new agent workspace checks.
What was not tested: No runtime sandbox/tool enforcement was tested because this PR only adds config-level policy conformance.

### Redacted CLI evidence

Malformed policy:

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "checksRun": 31,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "policy/policy-jsonc-invalid",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "policy.jsonc agents.workspace.allowedAccess must be an array.",
      "target": "oc://policy.jsonc/agents/workspace/allowedAccess"
    }
  ]
}
```

Nonconforming config:

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "checksRun": 31,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "policy/agents-workspace-access-denied",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "agents.defaults sandbox workspaceAccess 'rw' is not allowed by policy.",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/agents/defaults/sandbox/workspaceAccess",
      "requirement": "oc://policy.jsonc/agents/workspace/allowedAccess"
    },
    {
      "checkId": "policy/agents-tool-not-denied",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "agents.defaults does not deny required tool 'apply_patch'.",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/tools/deny",

}
```

Accepted read-only posture:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "checksRun": 31,
  "findings": [],
  "agentWorkspace": [
    {
      "id": "agents-defaults-workspace-access",
```

commands.author: alkor2000

target.title: fix(plugins): prevent openclaw update from silently downgrading managed deps
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #85184. Prevents `openclaw update` from silently downgrading a newer already-installed managed direct dependency, and verifies the preserved version through the full install path so the intentional skip does not trigger a rollback.

## What changed

- Add a narrow downgrade guard in installPluginFromManagedNpmRoot: during `update`, if the incoming version is older than the version already recorded in the managed npm root, keep the installed version instead of overwriting it.
- Verify the post-install result against the preserved version (not the older incoming resolution) when the guard fires, so the kept dependency is not misread as a failed install and rolled back.
- Scope is intentionally narrow: only the unambiguous downgrade case during `update`. Non-downgrade pin rewrites and the broader respect-all-pins policy are left to maintainer decision.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw update` re-synced externalized bundled-plugin direct dependencies through installPluginFromManagedNpmRoot, which overwrote the managed npm root dependency and could silently downgrade a newer pinned/installed version. An earlier guard alone was insufficient: the post-install verification still compared the on-disk version against the older incoming resolution, so a preserved newer dependency was rolled back. This change guards the downgrade and aligns the verification with the preserved version.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head. Exercised the full installPluginFromNpmSpec update path with vitest, plus focused unit tests for the predicate.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the plugin install/update test suite under the real runtime via vitest.

Evidence after fix: stdout from the test run against the real runtime:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/install.test.ts
  install.test.ts (120 tests)
  shouldPreserveManagedDependencyAgainstDowngrade
    + preserves a newer installed dependency when update would downgrade it
    + catches patch-level downgrades / allows upgrade / allows same version / etc.
  installPluginFromNpmSpec downgrade guard (integration)
    + preserves a newer installed managed dependency without rollback during update
    + allows a normal upgrade to rewrite the managed dependency (no false preserve)
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests  120 passed (120)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/update.test.ts src/infra/npm-managed-root.test.ts src/cli/update-cli.test.ts
  Test Files  3 passed (3)
  Tests  211 passed (211)
```

Observed result after fix: The integration test drives the full installPluginFromNpmSpec update path. With an installed 0.11.2 and an update resolving 0.10.0, the run completes successfully (no rollback) and the managed package.json keeps 0.11.2. A normal upgrade (installed 0.11.0, update 0.11.2) still rewrites to the newer version. All managed-root and update-cli acceptance suites pass unchanged.

What was not tested: A live mutating `openclaw update --tag` against a real registry was not run; the install/update path is exercised end to end with vitest using the real runtime and a mocked npm transport, matching the source-level reproduction in the issue.

target.title: perf(cli): skip config load for default root help
target.body:
## Summary
- avoid importing the full config/validation stack for `openclaw --help` when there is no config file, no OpenClaw dotenv file, and no plugin-help-affecting env override
- keep the existing live-config path for configured/plugin-sensitive help, invalid config handling, and injected-env tests
- include the legacy `~/.config/openclaw/gateway.env` runtime dotenv compatibility path in the fast-path precheck unless an explicit non-default `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` is active

## Why
The root help fast path has precomputed help text, but it still called `readConfigFileSnapshot()` first to see whether plugin config could affect help output. In a default/no-config invocation that dragged in config validation before printing static help.

## Real behavior proof
behavior: default `openclaw --help` startup no longer imports the full config/validation stack when no config, dotenv, or plugin-help-affecting env can change static root help output. Plugin-sensitive help still uses the live config path, including the legacy `~/.config/openclaw/gateway.env` compatibility file unless an explicit non-default `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` is active.

environment: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, CLI startup artifacts built with `node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`, isolated temporary HOME/state directories, real CLI startup measured with `node scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case help`.

steps:
1. Built CLI startup artifacts: `node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`.

3. Measured this PR's root help path with isolated temp-home runs: `node scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case help --runs 5 --warmup 0 --timeout-ms 10000 --json`.
4. Added regression coverage for legacy `~/.config/openclaw/gateway.env` plugin-sensitive env and explicit custom `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` exclusion.
5. Ran focused tests after the compatibility repair.

evidence:
```text
Before: node scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case help --runs 3 --warmup 0 --timeout-ms 10000 --json
avg duration 1116.7ms, avg RSS 264.1MB

After: node scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case help --runs 5 --warmup 0 --timeout-ms 10000 --json
avg duration 44.4ms, avg RSS 54.1MB

Focused regression: node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cli/root-help-live-config.test.ts
passed, 5/5

Diff hygiene: git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD -- src/cli/root-help-live-config.ts src/cli/root-help-live-config.test.ts
passed
```

observedResult: default root help improved by 1072.3ms (-96.0%) and 209.9MB RSS (-79.5%) in the isolated real CLI startup sample. The focused regression suite passes and covers both the legacy `gateway.env` compatibility path and the custom state-dir exclusion.

notTested: full repository CI was not run locally. GitHub Actions started on the pushed PR head; broad CI is still the merge gate.

## Tests
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cli/root-help-live-config.test.ts` - passed, 5/5 after the legacy `gateway.env` repair

target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Refs #77969
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

TTS can turn emoji and decorative symbols into noisy spoken names. Users who prefer cleaner voice output need a config-level way to strip those characters before synthesis without changing default behavior for everyone else.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `tts-skip-v3`, commit `06545b841d08`, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (`extensions/microsoft` -> `node-edge-tts` -> `speech.platform.bing.com`). No API keys or private account secrets were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... register microsoft plugin, call textToSpeech twice with skipEmojiSymbols=false/true, then call an emoji-only enabled case ... TS`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- After-fix evidence:
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # Registers the bundled Microsoft Speech plugin and performs real synthesis
  # through node-edge-tts / speech.platform.bing.com after this patch.
  TS
  {
    "branch": "tts-skip-v3",
    "commit": "06545b841d08",
    "provider": "microsoft",
    "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
    "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
    "observed": [
      {
  ```

- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing `✓`, `©`, and `🫠`; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same speech-core preprocessing path that all providers share.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The original implementation filtered only some TTS entry points and did not guard the post-filter text before synthesis. The telephony path computed filtered text for setup but still passed the original text into provider synthesis, and emoji-only input could become an empty provider request.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
  - stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
  - telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can enable `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` to strip emoji/symbol characters before speech synthesis. Unset or `false` preserves existing behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
text -> effective TTS config -> optional emoji/symbol strip -> empty guard -> provider synthesis
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: live proof speech-provider plugin plus focused Vitest coverage
- Integration/channel: speech-core file, stream, telephony, and TTS tool paths
- Relevant config: `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols=true|false`

### Steps

1. Run the live proof script shown above.
2. Run focused speech-core tests.
3. Run focused TTS tool tests.
4. Run test and production type gates.

### Expected

Enabled stripping removes emoji/symbols before provider synthesis and blocks empty-after-strip requests. Disabled mode preserves original text.

### Actual

The proof and tests match the expected behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Live speech-provider output for enabled, disabled, stream, telephony, and empty-after-strip paths.
- Focused speech-core test output.
- Focused TTS tool test output.
- Test and production typecheck output.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: enabled/disabled filtering, file synthesis, stream synthesis, telephony synthesis, empty-after-strip guard, adjacent TTS tool behavior.
- Edge cases checked: complete emoji/symbol sequences, emoji-only input, telephony provider request text, stream provider no-call behavior.
- Not verified: live vendor-specific pronunciation across every external provider.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: optional new config `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols`; no change required when unset.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Unicode stripping removes characters a user wanted spoken.
- Mitigation: behavior is opt-in and disabled by default.
- Risk: filtering differs across TTS paths.
- Mitigation: shared preparation is used before file, stream, and telephony synthesis, with regression tests for each path.

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.
